lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Where is the king?

lines.speaker: BEDFORD
lines.text: The king himself is rode to view their battle.

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: Of fighting men they have full threescore thousand.

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: There’s five to one; besides, they all are fresh.

lines.speaker: SALISBURY
lines.text: God’s arm strike with us! ’tis a fearful odds.

lines.speaker: SALISBURY
lines.text: God be wi’ you, princes all; I’ll to my charge:

lines.speaker: SALISBURY
lines.text: If we no more meet till we meet in heaven,

lines.speaker: SALISBURY
lines.text: Then, joyfully, my noble Lord of Bedford,

lines.speaker: SALISBURY
lines.text: My dear Lord Gloucester, and my good Lord Exeter,

lines.speaker: SALISBURY
lines.text: And my kind kinsman, warriors all, adieu!

lines.speaker: BEDFORD
lines.text: Farewell, good Salisbury; and good luck go with thee!

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Farewell, kind lord; fight valiantly today:

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: And yet I do thee wrong to mind thee of it,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: For thou art framed of the firm truth of valour.

lines.speaker: BEDFORD
lines.text: He is as full of valour as of kindness;

lines.speaker: BEDFORD
lines.text: Princely in both.

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: O that we now had here

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: But one ten thousand of those men in England

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: That do no work today!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: What’s he that wishes so?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: My cousin Westmoreland? No, my fair cousin:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: If we are mark’d to die, we are enow

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: To do our country loss; and if to live,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The fewer men, the greater share of honour.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: God’s will! I pray thee, wish not one man more.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: By Jove, I am not covetous for gold,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Nor care I who doth feed upon my cost;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: It yearns me not if men my garments wear;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Such outward things dwell not in my desires:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: But if it be a sin to covet honour,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I am the most offending soul alive.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: No, faith, my coz, wish not a man from England:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: God’s peace! I would not lose so great an honour

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: As one man more, methinks, would share from me

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: For the best hope I have. O, do not wish one more!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Rather proclaim it, Westmoreland, through my host,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That he which hath no stomach to this fight,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Let him depart; his passport shall be made

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And crowns for convoy put into his purse:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We would not die in that man’s company

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That fears his fellowship to die with us.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: This day is call’d the feast of Crispian:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: He that outlives this day, and comes safe home,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Will stand a tip-toe when this day is named,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And rouse him at the name of Crispian.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: He that shall live this day, and see old age,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Will yearly on the vigil feast his neighbours,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And say, “Tomorrow is Saint Crispian:”

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Then will he strip his sleeve and show his scars,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And say “These wounds I had on Crispian’s day.”

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Old men forget; yet all shall be forgot,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: But he’ll remember with advantages

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: What feats he did that day: then shall our names,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Familiar in his mouth as household words,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Harry the king, Bedford and Exeter,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Warwick and Talbot, Salisbury and Gloucester,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Be in their flowing cups freshly remember’d.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: This story shall the good man teach his son;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And Crispin Crispian shall ne’er go by,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: From this day to the ending of the world,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: But we in it shall be remembered;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We few, we happy few, we band of brothers;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: For he today that sheds his blood with me

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Shall be my brother; be he ne’er so vile,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: This day shall gentle his condition:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And gentlemen in England now abed

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Shall think themselves accursed they were not here,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And hold their manhoods cheap whiles any speaks

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That fought with us upon Saint Crispin’s day.

lines.speaker: SALISBURY
lines.text: My sovereign lord, bestow yourself with speed:

lines.speaker: SALISBURY
lines.text: The French are bravely in their battles set,

lines.speaker: SALISBURY
lines.text: And will with all expedience charge on us.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: All things are ready, if our minds be so.

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: Perish the man whose mind is backward now!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Thou dost not wish more help from England, coz?

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: God’s will! my liege, would you and I alone,

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: Without more help, could fight this royal battle!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Why, now thou hast unwish’d five thousand men;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Which likes me better than to wish us one.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: You know your places: God be with you all!

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: Once more I come to know of thee, King Harry,

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: If for thy ransom thou wilt now compound,

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: Before thy most assured overthrow:

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: For certainly thou art so near the gulf,

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: Thou needs must be englutted. Besides, in mercy,

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: The constable desires thee thou wilt mind

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: Thy followers of repentance; that their souls

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: May make a peaceful and a sweet retire

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: From off these fields, where, wretches, their poor bodies

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: Must lie and fester.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Who hath sent thee now?

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: The Constable of France.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I pray thee, bear my former answer back:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Bid them achieve me and then sell my bones.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Good God! why should they mock poor fellows thus?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The man that once did sell the lion’s skin

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: While the beast lived, was killed with hunting him.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: A many of our bodies shall no doubt

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Find native graves; upon the which, I trust,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Shall witness live in brass of this day’s work:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And those that leave their valiant bones in France,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Dying like men, though buried in your dunghills,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: They shall be famed; for there the sun shall greet them,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And draw their honours reeking up to heaven;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Leaving their earthly parts to choke your clime,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The smell whereof shall breed a plague in France.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Mark then abounding valour in our English,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That being dead, like to the bullet’s grazing,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Break out into a second course of mischief,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Killing in relapse of mortality.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Let me speak proudly: tell the constable

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We are but warriors for the working-day;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Our gayness and our gilt are all besmirch’d

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: With rainy marching in the painful field;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: There’s not a piece of feather in our host—

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Good argument, I hope, we will not fly—

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And time hath worn us into slovenry:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: But, by the mass, our hearts are in the trim;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And my poor soldiers tell me, yet ere night

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: They’ll be in fresher robes, or they will pluck

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The gay new coats o’er the French soldiers’ heads

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And turn them out of service. If they do this—

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: As, if God please, they shall—my ransom then

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Will soon be levied. Herald, save thou thy labour;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Come thou no more for ransom, gentle herald:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: They shall have none, I swear, but these my joints;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Which if they have as I will leave ’em them,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Shall yield them little, tell the constable.

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: I shall, King Harry. And so fare thee well:

lines.speaker: MONTJOY
lines.text: Thou never shalt hear herald any more.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I fear thou’lt once more come again for ransom.

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: My lord, most humbly on my knee I beg

lines.speaker: YORK
lines.text: The leading of the vaward.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Take it, brave York. Now, soldiers, march away:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And how thou pleasest, God, dispose the day!

stage_directions.text: Enter Gloucester, Bedford, Exeter, Erpingham, with all his host: Salisbury and Westmoreland.

glosses.definition: sixty thousand — Westmoreland's wildly inflated estimate of the French; the historical English/French ratio was closer to 1:4 or 1:5
five-to-one odds — Exeter's calculation; historically the French outnumbered the English roughly 3–5 to 1
terrifying numerical disadvantage

glosses.phrase: framed of the firm truth of valour
glosses.definition: made of solid, unflinching valour

glosses.phrase: ten thousand of those men in England
glosses.definition: Westmoreland wishes for reinforcements — the play's set-up line for Henry's reply
marked out for death
enough

glosses.phrase: fewer men, the greater share of honour
glosses.definition: Henry's keynote — fewer sharers means each gets more glory
by Jupiter — mild classical oath
doesn't grieve me — 'yearn' = grieve
if wanting honour is a sin — Henry's self-confessed ambition, the speech's personal note
cousin — affectionate contraction
Henry invites anyone unwilling to leave — a daring morale-setting gambit

glosses.phrase: hath no stomach to this fight
glosses.definition: has no appetite (or courage) for the battle
traveling documents will be provided — formal permission to leave
money for the journey home — 'crowns' = coins
St. Crispin's Day, October 25 — the date of Agincourt, 1415; Crispin and Crispianus (or Crispinian) were twin 3rd-century cobbler-saints martyred under Diocletian
will stand up on tiptoe (with pride) when this day is mentioned
the eve of a feast — the day before

glosses.phrase: strip his sleeve and show his scars
glosses.definition: the retired soldier's boast — the speech's physical gesture
remember with exaggerations — Henry's realism about aging memory

glosses.phrase: Familiar in his mouth as household words
glosses.definition: spoken as routinely as everyday expressions — the source of 'household words'

glosses.phrase: Harry the king, Bedford and Exeter
glosses.definition: Henry names his own company — binding them to himself in memory
remembered anew over the cups — the veterans drinking together
the twin saints' names paired — the speech's liturgical cadence

glosses.phrase: We few, we happy few, we band of brothers
glosses.definition: the speech's most-quoted line — 'band of brothers' becomes the foundational phrase of Anglo-American military fraternity (used for the WWII 101st Airborne, later the HBO series)
fights alongside me and bleeds with me
low-born; socially base — 'vile' in this period means low in rank, not morally evil
raise him to the rank of gentleman; make him noble — the promise of social elevation through shared combat, the speech's operative pledge
the nobles at home in bed — safe, and (Henry implies) less manly
they will curse themselves for missing this battle
they will feel their masculinity is worth less
October 25 — the battle's date, now bound to the speech
speed

glosses.phrase: Perish the man whose mind is backward now
glosses.definition: cursed be anyone who now hesitates
wished away 5,000 men by reducing the wished-for number
abyss; whirlpool
swallowed up
remind your soldiers to repent — Montjoy's grim courtesy before battle

glosses.phrase: achieve me and then sell my bones
glosses.definition: defeat me first, then profit from my corpse — Henry's sardonic answer

glosses.phrase: sell the lion’s skin / While the beast lived
glosses.definition: proverb — from Aesop's fable; don't count your victory before winning it

glosses.phrase: witness live in brass of this day’s work
glosses.definition: brass monuments will testify to this day's deeds

glosses.phrase: draw their honours reeking up to heaven
glosses.definition: raise their smoking honours to heaven — 'reeking' = steaming, smoking

glosses.phrase: smell whereof shall breed a plague
glosses.definition: English corpses rotting will poison France — a grotesque threat
a cannonball skipping along the ground and bouncing up to kill again
killing twice — once dying, once after death
workaday soldiers — Henry contrasts his ragged men with parade-ground finery

glosses.phrase: gayness and our gilt are all besmirch’d
glosses.definition: our bright colours and gilding are all tarnished
worn our appearance into slovenliness
hearts are in fighting condition — 'trim' = order, readiness
spare yourself the trouble; no more negotiations
command of the vanguard — the front division in battle

annotations.title: The speech's opening inversion — fewer, not more
annotations.body: The Crispin's Day oration begins with a reversal that does most of the speech's political work. Westmoreland wishes for reinforcements; Henry answers by wishing for even fewer men. 'If we are mark'd to die, we are enow / To do our country loss; and if to live, / The fewer men, the greater share of honour.' The rhetorical move is calculated: by publicly welcoming under-numbering, Henry takes the army's greatest liability (numerical inferiority) and converts it into the army's greatest asset (concentrated glory). The premise is that honour is a finite resource divided among the survivors, so a smaller share-base produces more per head. This is mathematically absurd and rhetorically irresistible. Westmoreland is immediately converted (line 74: 'Would you and I alone, / Without more help, could fight this royal battle!'). Henry now offers passports and pocket money to any man unwilling to fight; none leave. The opening inversion is the gate of the speech, and the audience feels it work.

annotations.title: 'Band of brothers' and the politics of fraternity
annotations.body: The core of the Crispin's Day speech — 'We few, we happy few, we band of brothers; / For he today that sheds his blood with me / Shall be my brother; be he ne'er so vile, / This day shall gentle his condition' — makes two enormous promises at once. The first is fraternity: the king will call the meanest soldier 'brother.' The second is social elevation: 'vile' here means low-born (not morally evil in this period), and 'gentle his condition' means to raise him to the rank of gentleman. Henry is offering, in effect, that shared combat will dissolve class distinction. The promise is explosive; it is also, historically, not kept. Agincourt's low-born survivors returned to England as commoners; very few were raised in rank. But the speech's rhetorical power is not damaged by its promise's thin fulfilment: the 'band of brothers' phrase has become foundational to English and American military rhetoric, used for the WWII 101st Airborne ('Easy Company'), later the HBO miniseries title, and across innumerable commemorations. The speech keeps working on any reading of Henry — patriotic or critical — because the promise of shared sacrifice and honour is, in the moment of battle, genuinely mobilizing. The fact that the speech works on the 'cynical' Henry as well as the 'heroic' one is itself part of the play's argument about rhetoric and power.

annotations.title: Henry's answer to Montjoy — the second, private oration
annotations.body: After the public Crispin's Day speech, Montjoy returns for one more attempt at ransom, and Henry delivers what is effectively a second oration, less quoted but equally tight. 'We are but warriors for the working-day; / Our gayness and our gilt are all besmirch'd / With rainy marching in the painful field.' The tonal register is entirely different from the high-heroic 'band of brothers' speech: plainer, grittier, almost proletarian ('working-day,' 'slovenry,' 'poor soldiers'). Henry offers the French the image of his army as an honest working crew in dirty uniforms. It is also a commitment not to be ransomed: 'they shall have none, I swear, but these my joints' — his body is the only ransom. The combination — a public oration that promises nobility and a private answer that promises only labour and death — is rhetorically masterful. Readers should notice how many registers Henry can speak in and how each is pitched for its audience.
sources: Brian Vickers, The Artistry of Shakespeare's Prose (1968)
Emrys Jones, Scenic Form in Shakespeare (1971)

trials.prompt: What does Henry do with Westmoreland's wish for ten thousand more men from England?
options: Grants it by sending a messenger
Inverts it — 'The fewer men, the greater share of honour' — converting the army's numerical liability into a rhetorical asset, then offering passports to anyone unwilling to fight
Rebukes Westmoreland publicly
Delays the battle to await reinforcements

trials.prompt: What does 'be he ne'er so vile, / This day shall gentle his condition' (lines 62–63) promise?
options: That cowards will be pardoned
That a low-born man ('vile' = socially base in period usage) who fights today will be raised to the rank of gentleman — a promise of social elevation through shared combat
That French prisoners will be treated well
That Henry's brothers will be made dukes

trials.prompt: How does 'band of brothers' work on the critical (as well as patriotic) reading of Henry?
options: It doesn't — cynical readings make the speech ineffective

The speech remains effective on any reading; even a calculating or cynical Henry could deliver it, and the rhetorical power of shared sacrifice doesn't depend on the speaker's sincerity. This is itself part of the play's argument about rhetoric and power
Only Olivier's 1944 film supports the patriotic reading
The speech is absent from critical editions

trials.prompt: What register does Henry use in his answer to Montjoy (lines 108–125)?
options: Same as the Crispin's Day oration
Plain, gritty, almost proletarian — 'warriors for the working-day,' 'besmirch'd' gilt, 'slovenry,' 'poor soldiers.' The tonal shift is pitched for the French herald
Outraged Latinate grandeur
Formal verse quatrains

trials.prompt: What date is St. Crispin's Day, and why does it matter?
options: June 15 — the anniversary of Henry's coronation
October 25 — the date of the Battle of Agincourt (1415); saints Crispin and Crispianus were 3rd-century martyred shoemakers
March 1 — St. David's Day
April 23 — St. George's Day

trials.prompt: Why is the Crispin's Day speech preceded by a second Montjoy-embassy (lines 79–88)?
options: To give Montjoy more lines
To give Henry a second oration-audience: the French herald gets the 'working-day' register, after the army has been given the 'band of brothers' register. The two audiences receive different speeches
Because Westmoreland requested it
Because Montjoy has changed sides
